html:
VIII

The Outlaws

It was a half-holiday and William was in his bedroom making careful preparations for the afternoon. On the mantelpiece stood in readiness half a cake (the result of a successful raid on the larder) and a bottle of licorice water. This beverage was made by shaking up a piece of licorice in water. It was much patronised by the band of Outlaws to which William belonged and which met secretly every half-holiday in a disused barn about a quarter of a mile from WilliamвАЩs house.

So far the Outlaws had limited their activities to wrestling matches, adventure seeking, and culinary operations. The week before, they had cooked two sausages which William had taken from the larder on cookвАЩs night out and had conveyed to the barn beneath his shirt and next his skin. Perhaps вАЬcookedвАЭ is too euphemistic a term. To be quite accurate, they had held the sausages over a smoking fire till completely blackened, and then consumed the charred remains with the utmost relish.

William put the bottle of licorice water in one pocket and the half cake in another and was preparing to leave the house in his usual stealthy fashionвБ†вАФthrough the bathroom window, down the scullery roof, and down the water-pipe hand over hand to the back garden. Even when unencumbered by the presence of a purloined half cake, William infinitely preferred this mode of exit to the simpler one of walking out of the front-door. As he came out on to the landing, however, he heard the sound of the opening and shutting of the hall door and of exuberant greetings in the hall.

вАЬOh! IвАЩm so glad youвАЩve come, dear. And is this the baby! The duck! Well, den, howвАЩs вАЩoo, den? GoвБ†вАФoвБ†вАФoo.вАЭ

This was WilliamвАЩs mother.

вАЬOh, crumbs!вАЭ said William and retreated hastily. He sat down on his bed to wait till the coast was clear. Soon came the sound of footsteps ascending the stairs.

вАЬOh, William,вАЭ said his mother, as she entered his room, вАЬMrs.¬†ButlerвАЩs come with her baby to spend the afternoon, and weвАЩd arranged to go out till teatime with the baby, but sheвАЩs got such a headache, IвАЩm insisting on her lying down for the afternoon in the drawing-room. But sheвАЩs so worried about the baby not getting out this nice afternoon.вАЭ

вАЬOh!вАЭ said William, without interest.

вАЬWell, cookвАЩs out and Emma has to get the tea and answer the door, and EthelвАЩs away, and I told Mrs.¬†Butler I was sure you wouldnвАЩt mind taking the baby out for a bit in the perambulator!вАЭ

William stared at her, speechless. The MedusaвАЩs classic expression of horror was as nothing to WilliamвАЩs at that moment. Then he moistened his lips and spoke in a hoarse voice.

вАЬMe?вАЭ he said. вАЬMe? Me take a baby out in a pram?вАЭ

вАЬWell, dear,вАЭ said his mother deprecatingly, вАЬI know itвАЩs your half holiday, but youвАЩd be out of doors getting the fresh air, which is the great thing. ItвАЩs a nice baby and a nice pram and not heavy to push, and Mrs.¬†Butler would be so grateful to you.вАЭ

вАЬYes, I should think sheвАЩd be that,вАЭ said William bitterly. вАЬSheвАЩd have a right to be that if I took the baby out in a pram.вАЭ

вАЬNow, William, IвАЩm sure youвАЩd like to help, and IвАЩm sure you wouldnвАЩt like your father to hear that you wouldnвАЩt even do a little thing like that for poor Mrs.¬†Butler. And sheвАЩs got such a headache.вАЭ

вАЬA little thing like that!вАЭ repeated William out of the bitterness of his soul.

But the Fates were closing round him. He was aware that he would know no peace till he had done the horrible thing demanded of him. Sorrowfully and reluctantly he bowed to the inevitable.

вАЬAll right,вАЭ he muttered, вАЬIвАЩll be down in a minute.вАЭ

He heard them fussing over the baby in the hall. Then he heard his elder brotherвАЩs voice.

вАЬYou surely donвАЩt mean to say, mother,вАЭ Robert was saying with the crushing superiority of eighteen, вАЬthat youвАЩre going to trust that child toвБ†вАФWilliam.вАЭ

вАЬWell,вАЭ said WilliamвАЩs mother, вАЬsomeone has to take him out. ItвАЩs such a lovely afternoon. IвАЩm sure itвАЩs very kind of William, on his half-holiday, too. And sheвАЩs got such a headache.вАЭ

вАЬWell, of course,вАЭ said Robert in the voice of one who washes his hands of all further responsibility, вАЬyou know William as well as I do.вАЭ

вАЬOh, dear!вАЭ sighed WilliamвАЩs mother. вАЬAnd everything so nicely settled, Robert, and you must come and find fault with it all. If you donвАЩt want William to take him out, will you take him out yourself?вАЭ

Robert retreated hastily to the dining-room and continued the conversation from a distance.

вАЬI donвАЩt want to take him out myselfвБ†вАФthanks very much, all the same! All I say isвБ†вАФyou know William as well as I do. IвАЩm not finding fault with anything. I simply am stating a fact.вАЭ

Then William came downstairs.

вАЬHere he is, dear, all ready for you, and you neednвАЩt go far awayвБ†вАФjust up and down the road, if you like, but stay out till teatime. HeвАЩs a dear little baby, isnвАЩt he? And isnвАЩt it a nice Willy-Billy den, to take it out a nice ta-ta, while itвАЩs mummy goes bye-byes, den?вАЭ

William blushed for pure shame.

He pushed the pram down to the end of the road and round the corner. In comparison with WilliamвАЩs feelings, the feelings of some of the early martyrs must have been pure bliss. A nice way for an Outlaw to spend the afternoon! He dreaded to meet any of his brother-outlaws, yet, irresistibly and as a magnet, their meeting-place attracted him. He wheeled the pram off the road and down the country lane towards the field which held their sacred barn. He stopped at the stile that led into the field and gazed wistfully across to the barn in the distance. The infant sat and sucked its thumb and stared at him. Finally it began to converse.

вАЬBlabвБ†вАФblabвБ†вАФblabвБ†вАФblabвБ†вАФblubвБ†вАФblubвБ†вАФblub!вАЭ

вАЬOh, you shut up!вАЭ said William crushingly.

Annoyed at the prolonged halt, it seized its pram cover, pulled it off its hooks, and threw it into the road. While William was picking it up, it threw the pillow on to his head. Then it chuckled. William began to conceive an active dislike of it. Suddenly the Great Idea came to him. His face cleared. He took a piece of string from his pocket and tied the pram carefully to the railings. Then, lifting the baby cautiously and gingerly out, he climbed the stile with it and set off across the fields towards the barn. He held the baby to his chest with both arms clasped tightly round its waist. Its feet dangled in the air. It occupied the time by kicking William in the stomach, pulling his hair, and putting its fingers in his eyes.

вАЬIt beats me,вАЭ panted William to himself, вАЬwhat people see in babies! ScratchinвАЩ anвАЩ kickinвАЩ and blindinвАЩ folks and pullinвАЩ their hair all out!вАЭ

When he entered the barn he was greeted by a sudden silence.

вАЬLook here!вАЭ began one outlaw in righteous indignation.

вАЬItвАЩs a kidnap,вАЭ said William, triumphantly. вАЬWeвАЩll get a ransom on it.вАЭ

They gazed at him in awed admiration. This was surely the cream of outlawry. He set the infant on the ground, where it toddled for a few steps and sat down suddenly and violently. It then stared fixedly at the tallest boy present and smiled seraphically.

вАЬDadвБ†вАФdadвБ†вАФdadвБ†вАФdadвБ†вАФdad!вАЭ

Douglas, the tallest boy, grinned sheepishly. вАЬIt thinks IвАЩm its father,вАЭ he explained complacently to the company.

вАЬWell,вАЭ said Henry, who was WilliamвАЩs rival for the leadership of the Outlaws, вАЬWhat do we do first? ThatвАЩs the question.вАЭ

вАЬIn books,вАЭ said the outlaw called Ginger, вАЬthey write a note to its people and say they want a ransom.вАЭ

вАЬWe wonвАЩt do thatвБ†вАФnot just yet,вАЭ said William hastily.

вАЬWell, itвАЩs not much sense holdinвАЩ somethinвАЩ up to ransom and not tellinвАЩ the folks that theyвАЩve got to pay nor nothinвАЩ, is it?вАЭ said Ginger with the final air of a man whose logic is unassailable.

вАЬNвБ†вАФoo,вАЭ said William. вАЬButвБ†вАФвАЭ with a gleam of hopeвБ†вАФвАЬwhoвАЩs got a paper and pencil? IвАЩm simply statinвАЩ a fact. WhoвАЩs got a paper and pencil?вАЭ

No one spoke.

вАЬOh, yes!вАЭ went on William in triumph. вАЬGo on! Write a note. Write a note without paper and pencil, and weвАЩll all watch. Huh!вАЭ

вАЬWell,вАЭ said Ginger sulkily, вАЬI donвАЩt sвАЩpose they had paper and pencils in outlaw days. They werenвАЩt invented. They wrote onвБ†вАФonвБ†вАФon leaves or something,вАЭ he ended vaguely.

вАЬWell, go on. Write on leaves,вАЭ said William still more triumphant. вАЬWeвАЩre not stoppinвАЩ you are we? IвАЩm simply statinвАЩ a fact. Write on leaves.вАЭ

They were interrupted by a yell of pain from Douglas. Flattered by the parental relations so promptly established by the baby, he had ventured to make its further acquaintance. With vague memories of his motherвАЩs treatment of infants, he had inserted a finger in its mouth. The infant happened to possess four front teeth, two upper and two lower, and they closed like a vice upon DouglasвАЩ finger. He was now examining the marks.

вАЬLook! Right deep down! See it? Wotcher think of that! Nearly to the bone! Pretty savage baby youвАЩve brought along,вАЭ he said to William.

вАЬI jolly well know that,вАЭ said William feelingly. вАЬItвАЩs your own fault for touching it. ItвАЩs all right if you leave it alone. Just donвАЩt touch it, thatвАЩs all. Anyway, itвАЩs mine, and I never said you could go fooling about with it, did I? It wouldnвАЩt bite me, I bet!вАЭ

вАЬWell, what about the ransom?вАЭ persisted Henry.

вАЬSomeone can go and tell its people and bring back the ransom,вАЭ suggested Ginger.

There was a short silence. Then Douglas took his injured finger from his mouth and asked pertinently:

вАЬWho?вАЭ

вАЬWilliam brought it,вАЭ suggested Henry.

вАЬYes, so I bet IвАЩve done my share.вАЭ

вАЬWell, whatвАЩs anyone else goinвАЩ to do, IвАЩd like to know? Go round to every house in this old place and ask if theyвАЩve had a baby taken off them and if theyвАЩd pay a ransom for it back? ThatвАЩs sense, isnвАЩt it? You know where you got it from, donвАЩt you, and you can go and get its ransom.вАЭ

вАЬI can, but IвАЩm not goinвАЩ to,вАЭ said William finally. вАЬIвАЩm simply statinвАЩ a fact. IвАЩm not goinвАЩ to. And if anyone says I darenвАЩt,вАЭ (glancing round pugnaciously) вАЬIвАЩll fight вАЩem for it.вАЭ

No one said he darenвАЩt. The fact was too patent to need stating. Henry hastily changed the subject.

вАЬAnyway, what have we brought for the feast?вАЭ

William produced his licorice water and half cake, Douglas two slices of raw ham and a dog biscuit, Ginger some popcorn and some cold boiled potatoes wrapped up in newspaper, Henry a cold apple dumpling and a small bottle of paraffin-oil.

вАЬI knew the wood would be wet after the rain. ItвАЩs to make the fire burn. ThatвАЩs sense, isnвАЩt it?вАЭ

вАЬOnly one thing to cook,вАЭ said Ginger sadly, looking at the slices of ham.

вАЬWe can cook up the potatoes and the dumpling. They donвАЩt look half enough cooked. LetвАЩs put them on the floor here, and go out for adventures first. All different ways and back in a quarter of an hour.вАЭ

The Outlaws generally spent part of the afternoon dispersed in search of adventure. So far they had wooed the Goddess of Danger chiefly by trespassing on the ground of irascible farmers in hopes of a chase which were generally fulfilled.

They deposited their store on the ground in a corner of the barn, and with a glance at the вАЬkidnap,вАЭ who was seated happily upon the floor engaged in chewing its hat-strings, they went out, carefully closing the door.

After a quarter of an hour Ginger and William arrived at the door simultaneously from opposite directions.

вАЬAny luck?вАЭ

вАЬNo.вАЭ

вАЬSame here. LetвАЩs start the old fire going.вАЭ

They opened the door and went in. The infant was sitting on the floor among the stores, or rather among what was left of the stores. There was paraffin-oil on its hair, face, arms, frock and feet. It was drenched in paraffin-oil. The empty bottle and its hat lay by its side. Mingled with the paraffin-oil all over its person was cold boiled potato. It was holding the apple-dumpling in its hand.

вАЬBall!вАЭ it announced ecstatically from behind its mask of potato and paraffin-oil.

They stood in silence for a minute. Then, вАЬWhoвАЩs going to make that fire burn now?вАЭ said Ginger, glaring at the empty bottle.

вАЬYes,вАЭ said William slowly, вАЬanвАЩ whoвАЩs goinвАЩ to take that baby home? IвАЩm simply statinвАЩ a fact. WhoвАЩs goinвАЩ to take that baby home?вАЭ

There was no doubt that when William condescended to adopt a phrase from any of his familyвАЩs vocabularies, he considerably overworked it.

вАЬWell, it did it itself. ItвАЩs no one elseвАЩs fault, is it?вАЭ

вАЬNo, itвАЩs not,вАЭ said William. вАЬBut thatвАЩs the sort of thing folks never see. Anyway, IвАЩm goinвАЩ to wash its face.вАЭ

вАЬWhat with?вАЭ

William took out his grimy handkerchief and advanced upon his prey. His bottle of licorice water was lying untouched in the corner. He took out the cork.

вАЬGoinвАЩ to wash it in that dirty stuff?вАЭ

вАЬItвАЩs made of waterвБ†вАФclean waterвБ†вАФI made it myself, so I bet I ought to know, oughtnвАЩt I? ThatвАЩs what folks wash in, isnвАЩt it?вБ†вАФclean water?вАЭ

вАЬYes,вАЭ bitterly, вАЬand what are we goinвАЩ to drink, IвАЩd like to know? YouвАЩd think that baby had got enough of our stuffвБ†вАФour potatoes and our apple-dumpling, anвАЩ our oilвБ†вАФwithout you goinвАЩ anвАЩ givinвАЩ it our licorice water as well.вАЭ

William was passing his handkerchief, moistened with licorice water, over the surface of the babyвАЩs face. The baby had caught a corner of it firmly between its teeth and refused to release it.

вАЬIf youвАЩd got to take this baby home like this,вАЭ he said, вАЬyou wouldnвАЩt be thinking much about drinking licorice water. IвАЩm simply statinвАЩвБ†вАФвАЭ

вАЬOh, shut up saying that!вАЭ said Ginger in sudden exasperation. вАЬIвАЩm sick of it.вАЭ

At that moment the door was flung open and in walked slowly a large cow closely followed by Henry and Douglas.

HenryвАЩs face was one triumphant beam. He felt that his prestige, eclipsed by WilliamвАЩs kidnapping coup, was restored.

вАЬIвАЩve brought a cow,вАЭ he announced, вАЬfetched it all the way from Farmer LittonвАЩs fieldвБ†вАФfive fields off, too, anвАЩ it took some fetching, too.вАЭ

вАЬWell, what for?вАЭ said William after a momentвАЩs silence.

Henry gave a superior laugh.

вАЬWhat for! YouвАЩve not read much about outlaws, I guess. They always drove in cattle from the surroundinвАЩ districks.вАЭ

вАЬWell, what for?вАЭ said William again, giving a tug at his handkerchief, which the infant still refused to release.

вАЬWellвБ†вАФerвБ†вАФwellвБ†вАФto kill anвАЩ roast, I suppose,вАЭ said Henry lamely.

вАЬWell, go on,вАЭ said William. вАЬKill it anвАЩ roast it. WeвАЩre not stoppinвАЩ you, are we? Kill it anвАЩ roast itвБ†вАФanвАЩ get hung for murder. I sвАЩpose itвАЩs murder to kill cows same as it is to kill peopleвБ†вАФвАЩcept for butchers.вАЭ

The cow advanced slowly and deprecatingly towards the вАЬkidnap,вАЭ who promptly dropped the handkerchief and beamed with joy.

вАЬBow-wow!вАЭ it said excitedly.

вАЬAnyway, letвАЩs get on with the feast,вАЭ said Douglas.

вАЬFeast!вАЭ echoed Ginger bitterly. вАЬFeast! Not much feast left! That baby William broughtвАЩs used all the paraffin-oil and potatoes, and itвАЩs squashed the apple-dumpling, and WilliamвАЩs washed its face in the licorice water.вАЭ

Henry gazed at it dispassionately and judicially.

вАЬYesвБ†вАФit looks like as if someone had washed it in licorice waterвБ†вАФand as if it had used up all the oil and potatoes. It doesnвАЩt look like as if it would fetch much ransom. You seem to have pretty well mucked it up.вАЭ

вАЬOh, shut up about the baby,вАЭ said William picking up his damp and now prune-coloured handkerchief. вАЬIвАЩm just about sick of it. Come on with the fire.вАЭ

They made a little pile of twigs in the field and began the process of lighting it.

вАЬI hope that cow wonвАЩt hurt the вАШkidnap,вАЩвАКвАЭ said Douglas suddenly. вАЬGo and see, William; itвАЩs your kidnap.вАЭ

вАЬWell, anвАЩ itвАЩs HenryвАЩs cow, and IвАЩm sorry for that cow if it tries playinвАЩ tricks on that baby.вАЭ

But he rose from his knees reluctantly, and threw open the barn door. The cow and the baby were still gazing admiringly at each other. From the cowвАЩs mouth at the end of a long, sodden ribbon, hung the chewed remains of the babyвАЩs hat. The baby was holding up the dog biscuit and crowed delightfully as the cow bent down its head and cautiously and gingerly smelt it. As William entered, the cow turned round and switched its tail against the babyвАЩs head. At the piercing howl that followed, the whole band of outlaws entered the barn.

вАЬWhat are you doing to the poor little thing?вАЭ said Douglas to William.

вАЬItвАЩs HenryвАЩs cow,вАЭ said William despairingly. вАЬIt hit it. Oh, go on, shut up! Do shut up.вАЭ

The howls redoubled.

вАЬYou brought it,вАЭ said Henry accusingly, raising his voice to be heard above the babyвАЩs fury and indignation. вАЬCanвАЩt you stop it? Not much sense taking babies about if you donвАЩt know how to stop вАЩem crying!вАЭ

The baby was now purple in the face.

The Outlaws stood around and watched it helplessly.

вАЬPвАЩraps itвАЩs hungry,вАЭ suggested Douglas.

He took up the half cake from the remains of the stores and held it out tentatively to the baby. The baby stopped crying suddenly.

вАЬDadвБ†вАФdadвБ†вАФdadвБ†вАФdadвБ†вАФdad,вАЭ it said tearfully.

Douglas blushed and grinned.

вАЬKeeps on thinking IвАЩm its father,вАЭ he said with conscious superiority. вАЬHere, like some cake?вАЭ

The baby broke off a handful and conveyed it to its mouth.

вАЬItвАЩs eating it,вАЭ cried Douglas in shrill excitement. After thoroughly masticating it, however, the baby repented of its condescension and ejected the mouthful in several instalments.

William blushed for it.

вАЬOh, come on, letвАЩs go and look at the fire,вАЭ he said weakly.

They left the barn and returned to the scene of the fire-lighting. The cow, still swinging the remains of the babyвАЩs hat from its mouth, was standing with its front feet firmly planted on the remains of what had been a promising fire.

вАЬLook!вАЭ cried William, in undisguised pleasure. вАЬLook at HenryвАЩs cow! Pretty nice sort of cow youвАЩve brought, Henry. Not much sense taking cows about if you canвАЩt stop them puttinвАЩ folksвАЩ fires out.вАЭ

After a heated argument, the Outlaws turned their attention to the cow. The cow refused to be вАЬshooвАЩd off.вАЭ It simply stood immovable and stared them out. Ginger approached cautiously and gave it a little push. It switched its tail into his eye and continued to munch the babyвАЩs hat-string. Upon WilliamвАЩs approaching it lowered its head, and William retreated hastily. At last they set off to collect some fresh wood and light a fresh fire. Soon they were blissfully consuming two blackened slices of ham, the popcorn, and what was left of the cake.

After the вАЬfeast,вАЭ Ginger and William, as Wild Indians, attacked the barn, which was defended by Douglas and Henry. The вАЬkidnapвАЭ crawled round inside on all fours, picking up any treasures it might come across en route and testing their effect on its palate.

Occasionally it carried on a conversation with its defenders, bringing with it a strong perfume of paraffin oil as it approached.

вАЬBlabвБ†вАФblabвБ†вАФblabвБ†вАФblabвБ†вАФblubвБ†вАФblubвБ†вАФDadвБ†вАФdadвБ†вАФdadвБ†вАФdadвБ†вАФdad. GoвБ†вАФoвБ†вАФoвБ†вАФo.вАЭ

William had insisted on a place on the attacking side.

вАЬI couldnвАЩt put any feelinвАЩ,вАЭ he explained, вАЬinto fightinвАЩ for that baby.вАЭ

When they finally decided to set off homewards, William gazed hopelessly at his charge. Its appearance defies description. For many years afterwards William associated babies in his mind with paraffin-oil and potato.

вАЬJust help me get the potato out of its hair,вАЭ he pleaded; вАЬnever mind the oil and the rest of it.вАЭ

вАЬMy hat! doesnвАЩt it smell funny!вБ†вАФand doesnвАЩt it look funnyвБ†вАФall oil and potato and bits of cake!вАЭ said Ginger.

вАЬOh! shut up about it,вАЭ said William irritably.

The cow followed them down to the stile and watched them sardonically as they climbed it.

вАЬBow-wow!вАЭ murmured the baby in affectionate farewell.

William looked wildly round for the pram, butвБ†вАФthe pram was goneвБ†вАФonly the piece of string dangled from the railings.

вАЬCrumbs!вАЭ said William, вАЬTalk about bad luck! IвАЩm simply statinвАЩ a fact. Talk about bad luck!вАЭ

At that minute the pram appeared, charging down the hill at full speed with a cargo of small boys. At the bottom of the hill it overturned into a ditch accompanied by its cargo. To judge from its appearance, it had passed the afternoon performing the operation.

вАЬThatвАЩs my pram!вАЭ said William to the cargo, as it emerged, joyfully, from the ditch.

вАЬGarn! SвАЩours! We found it.вАЭ

вАЬWell, I left it there.вАЭ

вАЬCome on! WeвАЩll fight for it,вАЭ said Ginger, rolling up his sleeves in a businesslike manner. The other Outlaws followed his example. The pramвАЩs cargo eyed them appraisingly.

вАЬOh, all right! Take your rotten old pram!вАЭ they said at last.

Douglas placed the baby in its seat and William thoughtfully put up the hood to shield his charge as far as possible from the curious gaze of the passersby. His charge was now chewing the pram cover and talking excitedly to itself. With a вАЬheart steeled for any fateвАЭ William turned the corner into his own road. The babyвАЩs mother was standing at his gate.

вАЬThere you are!вАЭ she called. вАЬI was getting quite anxious. Thank you so much, dear.вАЭ

But that is what she said before she saw the baby!
